levels.description: Pod backend-pod is Running but the Service backend-service has no endpoints. Requests to the Service are failing. Run kubectl get endpoints backend-service -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Empty endpoints always mean the Service selector matched zero pods. Start there:

  kubectl get endpoints backend-service -n k8smissions

Then compare the Service selector with the pod labels:

  kubectl get pod backend-pod -n k8smissions --show-labels
levels.hint2:
The pod has label `app: backend`. Now check what the Service selector requires:

  kubectl get service backend-service -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'selector'

Do the values match exactly — including spacing and capitalization?
levels.hint3:
The Service selector has `app: backend-app`. The pod has `app: backend`. One extra word — the selector matches nothing.

Find the selector field inside the Service spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service selector requires `app: backend-app` but the pod's label is `app: backend`. Labels are compared as exact string matches — `backend` and `backend-app` are different values. The Service has zero endpoints.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, under the Service spec:

```yaml
selector:
  app: backend
  tier: api
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes does not warn you when a Service selector matches nothing — it just returns an empty endpoints list. Always verify with `kubectl get endpoints` after creating a Service. If the list is empty, compare selector to pod labels character by character.

levels.description: A NodePort Service web-nodeport was created for external access, but nobody knows which port to connect to — and Kubernetes may assign a different one each time. Run kubectl get service web-nodeport -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Look at the PORT(S) column in the Service output. The nodePort is shown after the colon. Then check if it is in the valid NodePort range:

  kubectl get service web-nodeport -n k8smissions

Is the port stable and predictable, or randomly assigned?
levels.hint2:
Without an explicit `nodePort` field, Kubernetes assigns a random port from the 30000–32767 range each time the Service is recreated. This makes it impossible to set up consistent firewall rules or tell users where to connect.

Check what the Service spec looks like:

  kubectl get service web-nodeport -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ports'
levels.hint3:
The Service spec has no `nodePort` field set — Kubernetes picked one at random. You need to pin it to a specific value in the 30000–32767 range.

Find the ports section in manifest.yaml and add the explicit nodePort.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

No `nodePort` was specified. Kubernetes assigns a random available port in the 30000–32767 range. This changes if the Service is deleted and recreated, breaking any external configuration that expected a specific port.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add an explicit nodePort:

```yaml
ports:
- protocol: TCP
  port: 80
  targetPort: 80
  nodePort: 30080
```

## NodePort valid range

NodePorts must be in the range 30000–32767. Below 30000 is reserved for system services. Above 32767 is not allocated for NodePorts. If you try to set a port outside this range, the API server will reject it with a validation error.

levels.description: Pod app-client tries to reach the database but keeps printing 'Connection failed'. Both pods are Running. Run kubectl logs app-client -n k8smissions and read what hostname it is trying to reach.
levels.hint1:
The log shows the app is connecting to host `database`. Read what resources actually exist:

  kubectl get pods,services -n k8smissions

Look at the NAME column for each resource type. What is the difference between a pod name and a service name?
levels.hint2:
There is a pod named `database` and a Service named `database-service`. CoreDNS creates DNS records for **Services**, not for individual pods. So `database` (pod name) does not resolve — but `database-service` (service name) does.

Verify DNS resolution from inside the cluster:

  kubectl exec app-client -n k8smissions -- nslookup database-service
levels.hint3:
The client pod's command in manifest.yaml uses `-h database` (the pod name). It needs to use `-h database-service` (the Service name) instead.

Find the args field in spec.containers[0] of the app-client pod in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The app tries to connect to the hostname `database`, which is the pod's name. CoreDNS does not create DNS entries for pod names (unless configured separately). It only creates entries for Services. The Service is named `database-service`, so that is the correct hostname to use.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the app-client command:

```yaml
args:
- |
  while true; do
    pg_isready -h database-service -p 5432 || echo "Connection failed"
    sleep 10
  done
```

## How Kubernetes DNS works

When you create a Service, CoreDNS registers: `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. Within the same namespace, the short name `<service-name>` also resolves. Pod names are never registered in DNS unless you use a headless Service.

levels.description: An Ingress and Service both exist, the pod is Running, but every request returns 404. Run kubectl get ingress web-ingress -n k8smissions and look at the HOSTS and PATH columns.
levels.hint1:
A 404 from an Ingress controller usually means the request matched the host rule but not any path rule — so the Ingress has no rule for what was requested.

See what paths are configured:

  kubectl describe ingress web-ingress -n k8smissions

Look at the Rules section — what path is the Ingress listening on?
levels.hint2:
The Ingress rule shows path `/api`. But nginx by default only serves content at `/` (and its subdirectories). A request to `http://myapp.local/` does not match the Ingress rule `/api` — so the Ingress returns 404.

Check what paths nginx actually responds to:

  kubectl port-forward pod/web-app 8080:80 -n k8smissions
  curl http://localhost:8080/
levels.hint3:
The pod responds at `/` but the Ingress only routes requests to `/api`. No traffic from the root path ever reaches the backend.

Find `spec.rules[0].http.paths[0].path` in the Ingress in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Ingress rule has `path: /api` with `pathType: Prefix`. This means only requests whose URL path starts with `/api` are routed to the backend. Any request to `/` returns 404 from the Ingress controller, because no rule matches.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the Ingress path:

```yaml
paths:
- path: /
  pathType: Prefix
  backend:
    service:
      name: web-service
      port:
        number: 80
```

## pathType values

- `Prefix` — matches the path and any subpath (e.g. `/api` matches `/api/users`)
- `Exact` — matches only that exact path
- `ImplementationSpecific` — behavior depends on the Ingress controller

levels.description: Pod frontend is Running and trying to reach the backend API every 5 seconds. Pod backend is Running. But requests are being silently dropped. Run kubectl logs frontend -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
When packets are silently dropped (no connection refused, just timeout), a NetworkPolicy is usually responsible. Check if any NetworkPolicies exist in the namespace:

  kubectl get networkpolicy -n k8smissions

If one exists, read it:

  kubectl describe networkpolicy backend-network-policy -n k8smissions
levels.hint2:
The NetworkPolicy protects the backend pod and has an ingress rule that only allows traffic from pods with label `app: admin-tool`. Check the frontend pod's labels:

  kubectl get pod frontend -n k8smissions --show-labels

Does the frontend have that label?
levels.hint3:
The frontend has label `app: frontend` — not `app: admin-tool`. The NetworkPolicy ingress rule allows `admin-tool` but the actual caller is `frontend`. The labels do not match.

Find the podSelector inside the ingress rule in the NetworkPolicy in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The NetworkPolicy has `podSelector: {app: admin-tool}` in its ingress rule. This allows traffic only from pods labelled `admin-tool`. The frontend pod has `app: frontend` — it does not match. Kubernetes silently drops all packets from frontend to backend.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the NetworkPolicy ingress rule:

```yaml
ingress:
- from:
  - podSelector:
      matchLabels:
        app: frontend
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 8080
```

## Why packets are dropped silently

NetworkPolicy drop is not a TCP RST (connection refused) — it is an iptables DROP. The sender sees a timeout, not a refusal. This makes NetworkPolicy misconfigurations harder to spot than port or selector errors.

levels.description: Users are randomly getting logged out. The app stores sessions in memory — each pod has its own session store. Run kubectl logs client -n k8smissions and watch which pod each request hits.
levels.hint1:
Read the client logs to see which backend pod is responding to each request:

  kubectl logs client -n k8smissions

Do consecutive requests always go to the same pod, or do they alternate between 'Session Pod 1', 'Session Pod 2', 'Session Pod 3'?
levels.hint2:
Each request goes to a different pod. The Service load-balances randomly across all three. Since each pod has its own in-memory session store, a user logged into Pod 1 gets their session dropped when the next request hits Pod 2.

Check the Service spec:

  kubectl get service session-service -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'sessionAffinity'
levels.hint3:
There is no `sessionAffinity` field set — or it defaults to `None`. Without it, Kubernetes distributes requests round-robin across all endpoints.

You need to add `sessionAffinity: ClientIP` to the Service spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service uses the default `sessionAffinity: None`, which load-balances each request independently across all pods. A stateful app that stores sessions in memory requires the same client to always reach the same pod.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add to the Service spec:

```yaml
sessionAffinity: ClientIP
sessionAffinityConfig:
  clientIP:
    timeoutSeconds: 10800
```

## The better long-term fix

ClientIP affinity breaks if the client changes IP (mobile networks, proxies). The production solution is to store sessions outside the pods — in Redis or a database. Then any pod can serve any request and statelessness is preserved. This level shows why stateless applications are easier to scale.

levels.description: Pod frontend-app in k8smissions namespace is trying to reach an API in a different namespace. The connection always fails. Run kubectl logs frontend-app -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The log shows 'nslookup: can't resolve api-service'. The client is using the short name `api-service`. Check which namespace that Service actually lives in:

  kubectl get services --all-namespaces | grep api-service

Is it in k8smissions or somewhere else?
levels.hint2:
The Service is in `backend-ns`, not in `k8smissions`. Short DNS names (like `api-service`) only resolve within the same namespace. To reach a Service in another namespace, you must use the fully-qualified DNS name.

Test the FQDN format from inside the cluster:

  kubectl exec frontend-app -n k8smissions -- nslookup api-service.backend-ns.svc.cluster.local
levels.hint3:
The frontend-app pod's command uses `http://api-service` — which CoreDNS cannot resolve from k8smissions namespace. The URL in the command needs to be the full address.

Find the command field in spec.containers[0] of the frontend-app pod in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

CoreDNS resolves short names by appending the pod's own namespace: `api-service` becomes `api-service.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local`. No such Service exists there — it is in `backend-ns`. The lookup fails.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the frontend-app command to use the FQDN:

```yaml
command: ['sh', '-c', 'while true; do wget -q -O- http://api-service.backend-ns.svc.cluster.local 2>&1; sleep 5; done']
```

## DNS format for cross-namespace access

```
<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
```

This format always works regardless of which namespace the caller is in. Short names only work within the same namespace.

levels.description: Service web-service shows endpoints, but the client pod is still logging failures on some requests. Run kubectl get endpoints web-service -n k8smissions and then kubectl logs client -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Endpoints exist, so the Service found the pods. But some requests still fail. This points to pods being added to the endpoint list before they are actually ready to serve traffic.

Check the READY column for the pods:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=web

Are both pods showing 1/1 Ready?
levels.hint2:
The pods may show as Running but temporarily unready at startup. Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes adds the pod to the Service endpoints the moment the container process starts — even if the application inside is still initializing.

Verify whether readiness probes exist:

  kubectl get pod web-app-1 -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A8 'readinessProbe'
levels.hint3:
Neither pod has a readinessProbe. Both get added to Service endpoints the instant they start. If traffic arrives during a brief startup window, the request fails.

Add a readinessProbe to spec.containers[0] in both pod definitions in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

No `readinessProbe` is defined on either pod. Kubernetes marks a container Ready immediately after it starts — it has no way to know whether the application inside is actually handling requests. Traffic is sent to pods before they are ready, causing intermittent failures during restarts or rolling updates.

## Fix

Add a readinessProbe to each pod in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 5
  failureThreshold: 1
```

## Why this matters

Readiness probes are not optional for production workloads. Without one, every rolling update sends traffic to half-started pods. With one, Kubernetes waits for a passing health check before adding the pod to the Service endpoints.

levels.description: Service web-service was created as type LoadBalancer but EXTERNAL-IP has been showing as <pending> for several minutes. Run kubectl get service web-service -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
A LoadBalancer Service gets an external IP by asking the cloud provider (AWS, GCP, Azure) to provision a load balancer. In a local kind cluster, there is no cloud provider — so the request sits in pending forever.

Check what controller would normally respond:

  kubectl describe service web-service -n k8smissions

Look at Events — is anything trying to provision the load balancer?
levels.hint2:
Events are empty — no controller is watching. This is a local kind cluster with no cloud integration and no MetalLB installed.

For local development, the alternatives are:
- `type: NodePort` — exposes the service on a port on each node
- `kubectl port-forward` — tunnels a local port to the pod

Check which node IPs are available:

  kubectl get nodes -o wide
levels.hint3:
The Service needs to change from `type: LoadBalancer` to `type: NodePort` to work in this environment.

Find the `type` field in the Service spec in manifest.yaml. Also add an explicit `nodePort` value so the port is predictable.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`type: LoadBalancer` requires an external load balancer controller to respond to the Service creation and assign an IP. In cloud clusters (EKS, GKE, AKS), this happens automatically. In a local kind cluster, no such controller exists — the EXTERNAL-IP stays `<pending>` indefinitely.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the Service:

```yaml
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
    nodePort: 30080
```

Access it at `http://<node-ip>:30080`.

## When to use each type

- `ClusterIP` — internal only (default)
- `NodePort` — external access via node IP, works everywhere
- `LoadBalancer` — external access via cloud load balancer (requires cloud provider or MetalLB)
- `ExternalName` — maps to an external DNS name

levels.description: StatefulSet web has 3 pods running, but pods cannot resolve each other by hostname. The application expects to reach web-0 at web-0.web-cluster.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local. Run kubectl exec web-0 -n k8smissions -- nslookup web-0.web-cluster.
levels.hint1:
The nslookup fails. For StatefulSet pods to get individual DNS entries, the governing Service must be headless.

Check the current Service type:

  kubectl get service web-cluster -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'clusterIP'
levels.hint2:
The Service has a real `clusterIP` value (like `10.96.100.50`) — it is not headless. A regular ClusterIP Service gives you one virtual IP that load-balances across all pods. It does NOT create per-pod DNS records like `web-0.web-cluster`.

For per-pod DNS records, the Service needs `clusterIP: None`.

Note: you cannot patch a Service's clusterIP — you must delete and recreate it.
levels.hint3:
The Service needs `clusterIP: None` in its spec. Since you cannot change clusterIP in-place, you need to delete the existing Service and let your updated manifest.yaml recreate it as headless.

Delete first:

  kubectl delete service web-cluster -n k8smissions

Then fix the manifest and apply.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service has a real clusterIP. Kubernetes only creates per-pod DNS records (`web-0.web-cluster.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local`) when the Service is headless (`clusterIP: None`). With a regular ClusterIP Service, DNS only resolves the virtual IP — you cannot address individual pods by name.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the Service:

```yaml
spec:
  clusterIP: None   # headless
  selector:
    app: web-cluster
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
```

Before applying, delete the old Service:

```bash
kubectl delete service web-cluster -n k8smissions
```

Kubernetes does not allow changing clusterIP on an existing Service.

## What headless gives you

- `web-cluster.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local` → returns all pod IPs (round-robin)
- `web-0.web-cluster.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local` → returns exactly pod web-0's IP

levels.description: Pod web is Running and Service web-svc has endpoints. But every curl to the Service returns 'Connection refused'. Run kubectl get endpoints web-svc -n k8smissions to confirm endpoints are populated.
levels.hint1:
Endpoints exist — the Service found the pod by label. The problem is further down the routing path. Test the pod directly, bypassing the Service:

  kubectl port-forward pod/web 8080:80 -n k8smissions

Then in another terminal:

  curl http://localhost:8080

Does the pod respond?
levels.hint2:
The pod responds fine when accessed directly. So the problem is in how the Service forwards traffic to the pod. Check what port the Service is routing to:

  kubectl get service web-svc -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ports'

Then check what port the container actually listens on:

  kubectl get pod web -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'containerPort'
levels.hint3:
The container listens on port 80 (`containerPort: 80`). The Service has `targetPort: 8080`. Traffic arrives at the pod on port 8080, where nothing is listening — connection refused.

Find `spec.ports[0].targetPort` in the Service in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`targetPort: 8080` tells the Service to forward incoming traffic to port 8080 on the pod. But nginx listens on port 80. The kernel on the pod receives a connection on 8080, finds no listener, and sends TCP RST — connection refused.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, align targetPort with the container's actual port:

```yaml
ports:
- port: 80
  targetPort: 80
```

## Port fields in a Service

- `port` — what clients connect to on the Service's ClusterIP
- `targetPort` — what port traffic is forwarded to on the pod (must match containerPort or the named port)
- `nodePort` — (NodePort Services only) the port opened on every node's external interface

levels.description: All pods in the namespace suddenly cannot resolve any hostname. DNS lookups time out. Applications that used to work are now broken. Run kubectl exec <any-pod> -n k8smissions -- nslookup kubernetes.
levels.hint1:
DNS failures across all pods at once usually mean either CoreDNS is down or a NetworkPolicy is blocking outbound traffic to port 53.

Check if any NetworkPolicy exists in this namespace:

  kubectl get networkpolicy -n k8smissions

If one exists, read it carefully:

  kubectl describe networkpolicy deny-all-egress -n k8smissions
levels.hint2:
The NetworkPolicy selects all pods (`podSelector: {}`) and has policyType `Egress` with an empty `egress: []` list. In Kubernetes, when policyTypes includes Egress and egress rules are empty, ALL outbound traffic is blocked — including DNS queries to port 53.

CoreDNS listens on UDP and TCP port 53 in the kube-system namespace. Without an egress rule allowing this, pods cannot resolve any name.
levels.hint3:
The `egress: []` field needs to be replaced with rules that allow DNS traffic. At minimum, you need to permit outbound UDP and TCP on port 53.

Find the egress field in the NetworkPolicy in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`policyTypes: [Egress]` combined with `egress: []` is a total egress block. Every outbound packet from every pod is dropped — including DNS queries to kube-dns. Without DNS, service names cannot resolve, which breaks virtually every application.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, replace the empty egress list with rules that allow DNS and intra-namespace communication:

```yaml
egress:
- ports:
  - protocol: UDP
    port: 53
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 53
- to:
  - podSelector: {}
```

## The trap

When you add an Egress policy to block external traffic, you almost always need to explicitly allow DNS (port 53) in the same policy — otherwise all name resolution stops. This is one of the most common NetworkPolicy mistakes in production.

levels.description: NodePort Service nodeport-svc is running on the cluster. Connecting to one node works. Connecting to a different node returns nothing — the connection hangs and times out. Run kubectl get service nodeport-svc -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Intermittent failures depending on which node you hit point to `externalTrafficPolicy`. Check the full Service spec:

  kubectl describe service nodeport-svc -n k8smissions

Look for the External Traffic Policy line.
levels.hint2:
The policy shows `Local`. With `externalTrafficPolicy: Local`, the node only serves traffic if a matching pod is running on that same node. If you hit a node with no backend pod, the request is silently dropped — no error, just timeout.

Check which nodes have backend pods:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=backend -o wide

Are they on every node?
levels.hint3:
Backend pods are not on every node. Nodes without a pod silently drop incoming NodePort traffic when `externalTrafficPolicy: Local` is set.

Find the `externalTrafficPolicy` field in the Service in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`externalTrafficPolicy: Local` tells kube-proxy: only forward traffic if a matching pod is running on this node. If no pod is here, drop the packet. This is by design — it preserves the original client IP and avoids an extra network hop.

But in this cluster, pods are not on every node, so some nodes silently drop all NodePort traffic.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
externalTrafficPolicy: Cluster
```

With `Cluster`, any node receiving the traffic will forward it to a backend pod on another node if needed — no silent drops.

## Local vs Cluster trade-off

- `Cluster` — works everywhere, but loses original client IP and adds a network hop
- `Local` — preserves client IP, no extra hop, but requires pods on every node that might receive traffic (common with DaemonSets)

levels.description: Ingress app-ingress exists and the backend pod is Running. But every request to the Ingress returns 404. Run kubectl describe ingress app-ingress -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Read the Ingress rules output carefully:

  kubectl describe ingress app-ingress -n k8smissions

Look at the Backend column in the Rules section. Does the service name shown there match any Service that actually exists?
levels.hint2:
The Ingress backend references a Service named `app-serv`. Check what Services actually exist:

  kubectl get services -n k8smissions

Is there a Service named `app-serv`? Or is the real Service named something slightly different?
levels.hint3:
The Service is named `app-service` but the Ingress backend references `app-serv`. That is a typo — the Ingress controller cannot find the Service and returns 404 for every request.

Find `spec.rules[0].http.paths[0].backend.service.name` in the Ingress in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Ingress backend has `service.name: app-serv` but the Service is named `app-service`. The Ingress controller looked for `app-serv`, found nothing, and returned 404 for every request. Kubernetes does not warn you about this at creation time — it only becomes visible when traffic fails.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, correct the backend service name:

```yaml
backend:
  service:
    name: app-service
    port:
      number: 80
```

## Why Kubernetes doesn't reject it

Ingress resources are intentionally not validated against existing Services at admission time — Services may be created later. This flexibility means typos in service names cause silent 404 failures at runtime, not rejection at apply time.

levels.description: Service dual-svc exposes two ports and an Ingress routes traffic to it by port name. But the Ingress controller is rejecting the backend configuration. Run kubectl describe ingress dual-ingress -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The Ingress status shows an error about the backend port. Read the Ingress spec:

  kubectl get ingress dual-ingress -n k8smissions -o yaml

The backend references port by `name: http`. Now check the Service ports:

  kubectl get service dual-svc -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ports'
levels.hint2:
The Service has two ports (80 and 443) but neither has a `name` field. When an Ingress references a backend port by name (e.g. `name: http`), the Service port must have a matching name. Without names, the Ingress controller cannot find a port called `http`.

Note: for a Service with multiple ports, all ports must be named — it is required by the Kubernetes API.
levels.hint3:
Both Service ports need `name` fields. Port 80 should be named `http` and port 443 should be named `https` so the Ingress reference `name: http` can resolve correctly.

Find the ports section in the Service in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service exposes two ports but neither has a `name` field. When a Service has multiple ports, Kubernetes requires all ports to be named. The Ingress references the backend port as `name: http` — but with no names on the Service ports, the controller cannot find a match.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, add names to both Service ports:

```yaml
ports:
- name: http
  port: 80
  targetPort: 80
- name: https
  port: 443
  targetPort: 443
```

## Why multi-port Services need names

With a single port, Kubernetes can unambiguously identify which port you mean. With multiple ports, it cannot guess — names make the reference explicit. This is enforced by the API server: a multi-port Service without names will fail validation.
